---
title: "Moderated mediation with `pathXMY()`: the speeding EXSJT"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Moderated mediation with pathXMY(): the speeding EXSJT}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>")
```

This vignette demonstrates moderated mediation with `pathXMY()` using the
`speedingESJT` dataset â€” an **EXSJT** (Elaborated & eXperimental Situational
Judgment Test) in which a single driving scenario was randomly varied across
six factors, and each participant rated three speed-choice actions on eight
expected outcomes. Two moderators are examined: **time pressure** (a
situation-level factor randomly assigned to each respondent) and
**self-reported fast-driver tendency** (a person-level trait).

This contrasts with the design of `coworkerESJT`, in which each participant
rated 12 distinct scenarios. There, situational factors vary *within* person
across scenarios. In the speeding EXSJT each participant sees exactly one
fully crossed scenario, so the six situational factors vary *between*
persons. All moderators in this vignette therefore use `Z.within = FALSE`.

## The data

```{r data}
library(funfield)
data(speedingESJT)
lapply(speedingESJT, function(x) if (is.data.frame(x)) dim(x) else length(x))
```

The dataset is a named list with five components:

- **`$PSI`** â€” 999 rows: 333 persons Ă— 3 actions, with 8 expected-outcome
  ratings, the action's `Speed` coding, and the response `Likelihood`.
- **`$cond`** â€” the six randomly assigned situation factors per person,
  plus the assembled scenario text as displayed.
- **`$sit`** â€” situation perceptions (`Realistic`, `Relatable`) per person.
- **`$traits`** â€” 30 raw self-report items (`SR_01` â€¦ `SR_30`) plus
  demographics, per person.
- **`$codebook`** â€” survey wording, scale anchors, factor levels, and
  composite-scoring recipes.

All Likert ratings are stored on a [0, 1] proportion-of-maximum-possible
scale, so that within-person differences between two actions fall on
[-1, 1] â€” the natural metric for expectancy-value reasoning.

## Variables and survey wording

```{r wording-table}
knitr::kable(
  speedingESJT$codebook$outcomes[, c("var", "label")],
  col.names = c("Variable", "Survey wording"),
  caption = "Outcome items (PSI), rated on [0, 1]"
)
```

The focal **predictor** is `Speed` â€” the action's encoded speed level
(0 = at the limit, 0.5 = +5 mph, 1 = +10+ mph). The focal **outcome** is
`Likelihood` â€” how likely the respondent says they would take the action.
The eight **mediators** are the expected-outcome ratings.

## Data preparation

Within-person deviate all Level-1 columns. This is the only required
preprocessing â€” situation factors (in `$cond`) and traits (in `$traits`) are
already at the appropriate level and need no deviation.

```{r prep}
L1cols <- c("Speed","MoneyCost","FunDrive","IntQuality","Appropriate",
            "OnTime","Crash","Injured","Ticket","Likelihood")
mediators <- c("MoneyCost","FunDrive","IntQuality","Appropriate",
               "OnTime","Crash","Injured","Ticket")

dat <- speedingESJT$PSI
for (v in L1cols) {
  dat[[v]] <- ave(dat[[v]], dat$p, FUN = function(x) x - mean(x))
}
```

## Normative model (no moderator)

How does each expected outcome mediate the link between an action's speed
and how likely the respondent is to take it, averaging across people and
conditions?

```{r normative}
norm <- pathXMY(dat, X = "Speed", Y = "Likelihood", M = mediators)
norm$tidy
```

### Expectation paths (B1_MX): what is speeding expected to produce?

```{r bmx}
knitr::kable(
  subset(norm$tidy, param == "B1_MX")[, c("mediator","est","se","z","pvalue")],
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Expectation paths (Speed -> M)"
)
```

Faster speeds are expected to substantially increase `Ticket`, `Crash`,
`Injured`, `OnTime`, and (more modestly) `MoneyCost`, and to substantially
reduce perceived appropriateness (`Appropriate`). `FunDrive` and
`IntQuality` are essentially uncorrelated with `Speed` at the within-person
level â€” perceived enjoyment of the drive and quality of the next meeting do
not move much with the action's speed.

### Valuation paths (B1_YM): which expected outcomes drive action likelihood?

```{r bym}
knitr::kable(
  subset(norm$tidy, param == "B1_YM")[, c("mediator","est","se","z","pvalue")],
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Valuation paths (M -> Likelihood)"
)
```

`FunDrive` and `Appropriate` are the strongest positive drivers of whether
the respondent says they'd take the action, followed by `IntQuality` and
`OnTime`. `Crash`, `Injured`, `Ticket`, and `MoneyCost` all suppress
likelihood. The signs are intuitive throughout.

### Indirect effects (B1_MX Ă— B1_YM)

```{r bind}
knitr::kable(
  subset(norm$tidy, param == "B1_MX * B1_YM")[, c("mediator","est","se","z","pvalue")],
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Indirect effects through each mediator"
)
```

The pathways through `Ticket`, `Appropriate`, `Crash`, and `Injured` are
all large and negative: faster speeds are expected to produce tickets and
accidents (each of which strongly suppresses action likelihood) and to be
less appropriate (which also strongly suppresses likelihood). The `OnTime`
pathway is the lone strong positive: faster speeds are expected to get the
respondent to the meeting on time, which increases likelihood. `FunDrive`
and `IntQuality` contribute essentially nothing here â€” because faster
speeds don't shift those expectations, the pathways are near zero despite
their strong valuation paths.

## Situation-level moderation: time pressure

`timePressure` was randomly assigned at the person level â€” each respondent
saw exactly one of three levels (`comfortably ahead of schedule`,
`a bit late`, `quite late`). Code it as a continuous "lateness" variable
(0/0.5/1) and merge:

```{r prep-timepressure}
late_code <- c(`comfortably ahead of schedule` = 0,
               `a bit late`                    = 0.5,
               `quite late`                    = 1)
dat$late <- late_code[speedingESJT$cond$timePressure[match(dat$p,
                                                           speedingESJT$cond$p)]]

mod_s <- pathXMY(dat, X = "Speed", Y = "Likelihood",
                 M = mediators, Z = "late", Z.within = FALSE)
```

### Does time pressure moderate the expectation arm (Speed -> M)?

```{r bzmx-late}
knitr::kable(
  subset(mod_s$tidy, param == "BZ_MX")[, c("mediator","est","se","z","pvalue")],
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Moderation of expectation paths by time pressure (BZ_MX)"
)
```

### Does it carry through to the indirect effect?

```{r bz-indirect-late}
knitr::kable(
  subset(mod_s$tidy, param == "BZ_MX * B1_YM")[, c("mediator","est","se","z","pvalue")],
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Moderation of indirect effects by time pressure (BZ_MX x B1_YM)"
)
```

The largest BZ_MX is for `OnTime`: under time pressure, the expected
on-time gain from speeding is sharply amplified. The expected
meeting-quality gain (`IntQuality`) also strengthens. Other expectations
â€” including crash and ticket expectations â€” are not significantly
moderated by time pressure. People under time pressure don't expect
*different consequences* from speeding; they expect a *larger payoff* from
getting there on time.

The indirect-effect table propagates these shifts into the pull toward
speeding: under time pressure, the indirect path from Speed to Likelihood
strengthens through `OnTime` (the dominant route) and `IntQuality`. The
overall pull toward speeding rises with time pressure, not because the
costs feel smaller but because the on-time benefit feels bigger.

## Person-level moderation: self-reported fast-driver tendency

`SRFastDriver` is the [0, 1] rescaled response to *"I regularly drive far
over the speed limit when driving"* â€” item `SR_30` in `$traits`. As a
between-person trait, it uses `Z.within = FALSE`. Some respondents are
missing this item:

```{r prep-srfast}
sr <- speedingESJT$traits[, c("p","SR_30")]
sr <- sr[!is.na(sr$SR_30), ]
names(sr)[2] <- "SRFastDriver"
dat2 <- merge(dat, sr, by = "p")
cat("Persons with SRFastDriver:", length(unique(dat2$p)), "\n")

mod_p <- pathXMY(dat2, X = "Speed", Y = "Likelihood",
                 M = mediators, Z = "SRFastDriver", Z.within = FALSE)
```

### Does fast-driver tendency moderate the expectation arm?

```{r bz-mx-srfast}
knitr::kable(
  subset(mod_p$tidy, param == "BZ_MX")[, c("mediator","est","se","z","pvalue")],
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Moderation of expectation paths by SRFastDriver (BZ_MX)"
)
```

### Does it carry through to the indirect effect?

```{r bz-indirect-srfast}
knitr::kable(
  subset(mod_p$tidy, param == "BZ_MX * B1_YM")[, c("mediator","est","se","z","pvalue")],
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Moderation of indirect effects by SRFastDriver (BZ_MX x B1_YM)"
)
```

Self-described fast drivers expect speeding to be less catastrophic
(smaller `Crash`, `Injured`, `Ticket`, and `MoneyCost` shifts), more
enjoyable (`FunDrive`), and substantially more role-appropriate
(`Appropriate` â€” by far the largest effect). These expectation differences
propagate into significantly stronger indirect pulls toward taking the
faster action through every meaningful pathway: `Appropriate` (the largest),
`FunDrive`, `Crash`, `Injured`, and `Ticket`. The contrast with the
time-pressure result is informative: time pressure shifts the *upside*
(OnTime, IntQuality); fast-driver tendency shifts the *downside* (the
expected costs of speeding feel smaller, and the action feels more
appropriate).

## Scoring named trait composites

If you want the Big Five and other named scales from
Wood, Harms, & Cho (2023), use the recipes in
`speedingESJT$codebook$trait_scales`:

```{r scoring, eval = FALSE}
tr <- speedingESJT$traits
tr$Extra <- rowMeans(cbind(1 - tr$SR_15, tr$SR_16, tr$SR_17), na.rm = TRUE)
tr$Agree <- rowMeans(cbind(tr$SR_18, 1 - tr$SR_19, tr$SR_20), na.rm = TRUE)
tr$Consc <- rowMeans(cbind(1 - tr$SR_21, 1 - tr$SR_22, tr$SR_23), na.rm = TRUE)
tr$Neuro <- rowMeans(cbind(tr$SR_24, tr$SR_25, 1 - tr$SR_26), na.rm = TRUE)
tr$Open  <- rowMeans(cbind(tr$SR_27, 1 - tr$SR_28, tr$SR_29), na.rm = TRUE)
```

## Function reference

See `?pathXMY` and `?speedingESJT`.
